children.text: Being an Afro Peruvian woman means being descended from resilient women like La Tia Chicha, who believed education was a path to upward social mobility. She did not have the opportunities available after the agrarian reform in Guayabo. To me, her grandniece is pursuing a PhD at UT Austin. I am also descended from Mamaine, my lovely auntie, who, in the documentary, says she was always proud to be a Black woman. From Susana Matute, with whom I had the privilege of working on her team in my early 20s, who taught me the bravery it takes to work in a government space where your existence is constantly denied. To Victoria Santa Curz, who inspired my journey to develop my artistic career.

¿Qué cosa es ser negra?

Para mí, es descendiente de las mujeres más fuertes que, frente a la opresión, aún imaginaron nuevos mundos donde puedas construir tu propia definición de quién eres. Soy Pilar Cáceres Cartagena, llevando el apellido Cartagena muy orgullosamente desde las mujeres negras que no resistieron la trata trans atlántica, a las ancestras que llegaron al Perú, a las que resistieron la esclavitud, quienes pelearon por la abolición de esta, quienes estuvieron sometidas al
sistema de haciendas, quienes vieron la reforma agraria y con ella la ilusión de al fin acceder a las propiedades, quienes sobrevivieron a los fracasos de la reforma agraria, a las que migraron a Lima en busca de una oportunidad mejor, a las pocas que accedimos a la educación superior y por las mujeres afroperuanas que vengan. Por la lucha continua por un futuro sin racismo, ni discriminación.
Es tener herencia. Es tener afrodescendencia. Es resistencia.
What does it mean to be "Negra"?
It is heritage. It is afrodescedance. It is resistance.
The same way I wrote this song for all my

Pilar Caceres Cartagena (She/They) is an international Ph.D. student from Lima, Peru. She holds a B.A. in Sociology from the Universidad Nacional de San Marcos and an M.A. in African and African Diaspora Studies. Her research focuses on the intersections of Black Queer Studies, Afro-Latinx Studies, Hip Hop Studies, and music. Pilar is also an artist who uses poetry and music to narrate her experiences as a queer, black body in a discriminatory society, as is Peru. She is also a 2021 Ford Global Fellow for her artivism, has participated in the SUSI Program at the University of Arizona. Contact her at p.caceres@utexas.edu
